text.tspan: Question: 'I have a 200-page doc. Do I use RAG, or do I just stuff it in the context window?'
2024-2026 answer: both can work. Choice depends on size, query patterns, cost, latency.
Works for: small-to-medium corpora (under ~500k tokens).
Gemini 2.5 / Claude Sonnet 4: 1M-2M token windows.
+ No retrieval setup.
+ No retrieval misses (the model sees everything).
+ Pairs with PROMPT CACHING (90% cost reduction).
- Context rot above ~30-50k tokens.
- Expensive per call without caching.
- Slow first call (entire doc must be processed).
Works for: large corpora (>1M tokens), changing data.
+ Scales to billions of chunks.
+ Cheap per query (only K chunks in context).
+ Easy to update (re-embed new docs).
- Setup complexity (chunking, embedder, vector DB, reranker).
- Retrieval misses (relevant chunk not in top-K = wrong answer).
- Loss of holistic understanding.
Long-context for things that fit + need holistic view.
RAG for the long tail of large corpus.
CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation): long-context + prompt caching
= fits intermediate-sized cases cheaply.
Agentic RAG: the model decides when to retrieve vs trust its context.
Fits in 200k tokens + stable? -> LONG-CONTEXT + cache (CAG)
Fits in 1M tokens + needs aggregation? -> LONG-CONTEXT + cache
Bigger than 1M tokens or changing fast? -> RAG
Needs both breadth and depth? -> HYBRID
